source: Claude gap analysis 2026-06-11 (Track B — single biggest agentic upgrade)
title: Self-verification: root gets a run_verify tool and uses it after code edits
current_state: Pi can edit its own code (modify_file/create_file) but cannot test its own edits — only the human-run scripts/verify.py or sprint.py do. Pi claims fixes it never verified, which is the LOG1/LOG2 honesty class applied to code.
target_state: A run_verify ToolSpec executes 'python scripts/verify.py --quiet' in a subprocess (10-min timeout) and returns {overall: PASS|FAIL, syntax_failed: [...], gate_failures: [...], test_failures: [...], tail: last-40-lines}. A scoped variant run_tests(file) runs one pytest file for fast iteration. Consciousness gains one rule: after any tool edit to a .py file, run run_tests on the nearest test (or run_verify before claiming done) — never report a code fix without a green result in the same turn.
migration_plan: 1. ToolSpec run_verify in tools_project.py: subprocess verify.py --quiet, parse docs/STATUS.md Overall + failure blocks into the structured dict.
2. ToolSpec run_tests: subprocess 'python -m pytest testing/<file> -q --tb=short', 120s timeout, return code + tail.
3. Guard: refuse to run if another verify subprocess is already live (lock file).
4. Consciousness rule (one paragraph, in the existing honesty section): edit→test before claiming success.
5. Tests: mock subprocess — PASS and FAIL parse correctly; timeout returns structured error; lock prevents concurrent runs.
risk_notes: consciousness.txt is RISK_FLAGGED → interactive. Token cost: verify takes minutes — instruct the model to prefer scoped run_tests during iteration, full run_verify once at the end.
